data.title: AST SpaceMobile: Priced For Space Hype While Facing Constant Delays
data.description:
AST SpaceMobile, Inc. (ASTS) still hasn't launched the next-generation satellites with 10x the bandwidth capacity of the minimal existing satellites in space, yet the stock has surged during 2025. The space-based cellular broadband company won't offer continuous coverage until sometime, possibly not until 2027, assuming upcoming satellite launches occur per delayed plans. My investment thesis remains ultra Bearish on the stock continuing to fail upwards, though a retest of $100 is definitely possible.

A Year Of Delays

AST SpaceMobile had a very productive year outside of actually launching new Block 2 BlueBird satellites needed to really deploy commercial direct-to-device (DTD) services. The company has raised substantial cash, acquired spectrum, built satellites, and signed commercial agreements with sizable prepayments.

The company started 2025 with the following plan on the Q1'25 earnings release.

Today, we are at an inflection point for the company. We have ramped up manufacturing capacity and are now able to announce our plans to support five scheduled orbital launches over the next six to nine months.

Only a few weeks ago, AST SpaceMobile announced the BlueBird 6, a U.S. licensed satellite, was scheduled to launch on December 15th from the Satish Dhawan Space Center in India. Just today, news has surfaced that the launch date has already been pushed back to December 20.

The reported delay isn't huge this time at only 5 days, but investors should still have lots of questions on the confidence of this launch timeline. Remember, AST SpaceMobile needs the following satellites launched to cover services as follows:

  • 25 satellites to offer non-continuous coverage in main markets like US, Europe, and Japan.
  • 45-60 satellites to offer continuous coverage in main markets like US, Europe, and Japan.
  • 100 satellites to offer global continuous coverage.

The company plans 13 launches through the end of 2026 to reach the 45 to 60 satellite goal. AST SpaceMobile has 50 MNOs lined up with access to 3 billion customers, but the big question is how many subscribers actually sign up for a $10/monthly service that isn't necessary.

A lot of investors conveniently ignore that the former Verizon (VZ) CEO went on CNBC and claimed the D2D satellite service was great for covering national parks. The wireless giant is an investor and signed a definite commercial agreement back in October, so Verizon sees value in the satellite service.

Wild Revenue Estimates

While the market went wild this year, sending the stock to $100, huge questions exist on the revenue potential of the business. On the Q3'25 earnings call, CEO Abel Avellan disclosed the company has secured large revenue commitments from MNOs as follows:

Today, we're happy to disclose for our first time that we have secured over $1 billion in total contracted revenue commitment from our commercial partners.

Management isn't very clear on the period covered by this revenue commitment, not the amount of MNOs included in the amount. Based on the stc group agreement for Saudi Arabia for a contracted term of 10 years, one can assume this $1 billion revenue commitment is total over an extended period of possibly 5 to 10 years, not the annual necessary for stock analysis.

The consensus analysts estimates now forecast revenues of $254 million in 2026 and $882 million in 2027. The big revenue estimates for a few years from now have been significantly cut and seem irrationally high compared to total revenue commitments, with the combined 2026/27 revenue targets topping $1.1 billion now.

Market research only projects the satellite-to-phone service market reaching $11 billion by 2032, up from $6 billion last year. Starlink appears poised to grab a lot of the growth with the launch of the first full constellation of "Starlink Direct to Cell" satellites enabling voice services with T-Mobile (TMUS).

AST SpaceMobile now has a market cap of nearly $30 billion on the rally back above $80 while 2027 revenue targets trail $1 billion. The company has a lot of lingering questions about whether the business can really ramp up to large amounts approaching $1 billion annually, especially when the total customer commitments are only $1 billion over periods of up to 10 years.

The satellite company is looking at 80% to 90% gross margins with quarterly operating expenses now in the $65 million range and likely to rise as the company scales. Naturally, the biggest cost will be depreciation on the satellite system built for over $2.2 billion based on the goal of at least 100 satellites costing $22 million each.

AST SpaceMobile has now raised the cash balance to $3.2 billion, so the company has generally eliminated any of the original funding risks. The big question are the legitimate revenue targets, and the market is just far too aggressive on a product meant for 5G coverage gaps and with a massive competitor in Elon Musk and SpaceX.

Takeaway

The key investor takeaway is that investors should use this current rally and another potential move to $100 as an opportunity to again sell the stock for a huge premium. AST SpaceMobile still has a lot to prove on even launching satellites, much less generating revenues in the billions required to justify this stock price.

data.title: Academy Sports: Store Growth And Margins Power A Q3 Beat (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Shares of Academy Sports and Outdoors (ASO) have been a modest performer over the past year, gaining just about 5%. However, investors received good news on Tuesday when the company reported stronger Q3 earnings results, sending the stock up 8%. I last covered shares in September when I downgraded the stock to a “hold.” Since then, ASO has gained 7%, right in line with the market and consistent with my rating. With updated financials and an uncertain outlook for consumer spending, now is a good time to revisit shares.

In the company’s third quarter, Academy Sports and Outdoors earned $1.14, which beat estimates by $0.06 as revenue grew by 3% to $1.4 billion. Thanks to stronger margins and a reduced share count, EPS was up 16% from last year. While sales were up from last year, I would note that this is entirely due to new store openings, with same-store sales down by 0.9%. The company has been seeking to aggressively grow its store count both in its core Southeast US footprint as well as elsewhere in the country. With DICK’s (DKS) focused on integrating Foot Locker, and sneaker manufacturers in particular looking to avoid being overly reliant on DKS, I do believe that now is an ideal time for ASO’s expansion effort. Indeed, it launched the Jordan brand from NIKE (NKE) earlier this year.

Store openings accelerate, creating temporary margin pressure

During the quarter, Academy opened 11 new stores. This was a nice acceleration from the 8 opened in the first half of the year, and its store count is up 6% from last year. The typical new store provides over $12 million in first-year sales, so these openings should support ~$140-150 million in incremental sales over the next year. The company should open about 25 stores this year, and I expect at least 22 more stores next year, providing a clear trajectory for further revenue growth even in a muted consumer spending environment. Given its comparatively small footprint, I do not see ASO as being at risk of over-saturating the market.

In Q3, gross margins were up 1.7% to 35.7%. Given the pressure from tariffs, I view this margin performance as especially strong. Now, SG&A as a share of sales did rise by 120 bps to 28.4%, offsetting some of this gross margin expansion. I view this issue as largely transitory. When new stores open, it takes time for sales to ramp up to normal levels, but staffing costs ramp up more quickly. This creates a temporary margin pressure, and then as the new stores get to a run-rate footing, margins recover. With store openings accelerating in Q3, this is creating a temporary pressure, which should gradually ease.

One risk to watch is that inventories are up over 11% to $1.7 billion, which outpaces sales growth by ~800 bps. I generally like to see inventory rise no faster than sales, as excess inventory growth can lead to future promotional activity and reduced margins. In this case, we are seeing no margin pressure—the reverse is occurring. Like many retailers, ASO accelerated orders to front-run tariffs, temporarily boosting inventories. Plus, similar to the SG&A issue, it needs to stock new stores before they start contributing sales. Given these factors, I am comfortable with the inventory position but expect some inventory normalization over the next 6-9 months.

Balance sheet and cash flow enable buybacks

ASO is also a highly cash-generative business. So far this year, Academy Sports has generated $115 million of free cash flow, or $93 million adding back share-based compensation. Working capital has been a $117 million headwind this year, due to both seasonal factors and the need to stock new stores with inventory. I would expect a portion, but not all, of this working capital pressure to reverse in the fourth quarter.

Importantly, Academy maintains a strong balance sheet. It has $289 million of cash and just $481 million of long-term debt. Aside from debt, it does have $1.4 billion of lease liabilities, which are debt-like in nature. As such, it is appropriate for the company to maintain low debt levels. Given this balance sheet, I view its 1% dividend yield as very secure, and I expect ASO to use free cash flow to buy back stock, as there is no pressing need for further debt reduction. Indeed, thanks to share buybacks, its share count was down about 5.4% from last year.

Guidance progressed as expected

Alongside results, ASO tightened guidance while maintaining similar midpoints. This largely reflects the fact that Q3 went as anticipated, and there is now less variance for the remainder of the year, given there is just one quarter left. Management also noted that the holiday season is performing as expected, with Black Friday sales reaching a record. Same-store sales are set to be down about 1% in my view from last year. I was particularly heartened by the tightening of the gross margin outlook to 34.3-34.5%, and margin performance has been a clear strong spot.

Back in September, I was forecasting $5.30-$5.60 in EPS. I prefer to focus on GAAP EPS rather than adjusted EPS, which excludes share-based compensation. While a common practice, I view share-based compensation as a real cost given the resultant dilution for shareholders. I now see the company likely earning $5.40-$5.70 this year, thanks to its stronger gross margin performance and resilient performance in the face of tariff pressures.

Risks are manageable

As with any investment, Academy Sports carries risk. From a macro perspective, slowing consumer spending would be a pressure given the discretionary nature of sporting goods. With several Fed rate cuts and a larger tax refund season coming (given how the July tax bill was constructed), I expect consumer spending to stabilize or even pick up slightly in coming months. However, if this view is wrong and the labor market rolls over, that would likely pressure sales and ASO's share price.

Operationally, ASO is meaningfully growing its store count, at 5-7%/year. I view this as a manageable pace of growth, but if construction costs were to rise or ASO were to overbuild, that could pressure margins more structurally and risk cannibalizing existing sales. Thus far, we are not seeing evidence of this, and given its relatively small footprint, I do not see this being a material challenge for several years.

Shares are attractive given forward EPS growth

Looking forward to 2026, I expect about a ~5% benefit from store openings and a ~4% benefit from share count reduction. Assuming flat to slightly down same-store sales, I see the company generating $5.85-$6.20 in GAAP EPS next year. Given muted same-store sales growth but a solid balance sheet and organic expansion opportunity, I view ~10x earnings as a reasonable target, or ~$60 over the next 12 months.

This 12-month target points to ~14% total return upside. With its margin performance outperforming my expectations and having an ideal opportunity to grow its store count, I believe after the market performance of the past few months, ASO is now better positioned to outperform the market going forward. As such, I am upgrading shares back to a “buy” and expect the solid Q3 earnings report to be a sign of things to come.

data.title: Built To Last? Understanding The Foundation Supporting The Growth Of AI
data.description:
Over the past several years, AI has driven a powerful investment cycle, and companies viewed as AI leaders have seen their stock prices move higher. While today’s AI enthusiasm shares some characteristics with prior technology-related bubbles, such as elevated market concentration and valuations among perceived winners, we view this cycle as more grounded.

However, as the AI landscape continues to evolve, companies viewed as winners today may not hold that position in the future. Often, the most transformative beneficiaries are those not yet on investors’ radar.

For example, during the early stages of the internet buildout in the late 1990s, almost no one was focused on Alphabet (GOOG) (GOOGL) or Netflix (NFLX), and Meta Platforms (META) had yet to form as a company, yet each went on to benefit from the development of the internet.

We believe our quality growth investment approach, with a focus on identifying structurally advantaged companies through rigorous bottom-up research, provides the foundation to help identify the potential winners today - and in the future - as AI adoption continues to advance.

A More Durable AI Cycle

The current AI buildout is supported by meaningful technological advances and real-world adoption, suggesting a more durable foundation for growth. However, there will likely be periods of consolidation along the way.

We believe maintaining balance in our large-cap growth portfolio, participating selectively where we see fundamentals supporting growth and valuation supporting future stock performance, is a prudent approach.

In this nascent stage, AI technologies are already being deployed to improve productivity, automate processes, and enhance decision-making across a wide range of industries, including healthcare, manufacturing, software, and financial services. The scale of corporate investment and the tangible business benefits being realized suggest that while investor sentiment is elevated, it is not entirely disconnected from fundamentals.

Why This Doesn’t Look Like 1999

We view the current environment as distinct from the internet bubble of 1999. Today’s companies investing in the buildout of AI infrastructure are mostly generating substantial free cash flow and funding growth organically. In contrast, the 1999 internet bubble was fueled largely by capital markets, with many businesses reliant on external financing and unsustainable business models that were not generating a profit.

Back in the late 1990s, a large amount of capital was being used to install dark fiber, which refers to the large volume of unused fiber-optic cables laid during the early internet buildout - capacity that far exceeded actual demand.

While this overbuild of capacity eventually proved useful, it initially led to poor returns on invested capital as telecom companies poured billions into infrastructure that sat idle for many years.

Eventually, the capital markets shut down, driven by higher interest rates and lack of near-term revenues. This dynamic, coupled with low returns from these investments, became problematic.

In contrast, today’s AI cycle is seeing the opposite dynamic. Graphic processing units (GPUs), which are the foundation used for AI workloads, have been deployed at or near full utilization since day one, generating immediate productivity and revenue for AI developers and hyperscalers (i.e., large data center companies).

The comparison highlights a key difference between past and present technology investment cycles. Dark fiber was a case of supply racing ahead of demand, whereas modern AI infrastructure is a case of demand pulling supply forward, supporting higher initial returns on capital deployed.

In addition, the current investment cycle appears more durable, supported by companies that are generally profitable and reinvesting internally generated cash flow to support future growth. That said, select companies are accessing the capital markets to fund some of their AI-related growth initiatives - a dynamic we are closely monitoring.

The chart below highlights the capital expenditure (capex) growth among major hyperscalers investing in AI infrastructure. Recent earnings commentary suggests these figures could continue to increase.

AI Investments Are Driving Higher Capex

Capex for several mega-cap companies is projected to increase due to investments in AI, but these companies have the free cash flow to organically support growth investments.

Capex Capacity and Free Cash Flow Strength

Despite sizable capex commitments, these companies have free cash flow margins near 20%, underscoring their financial strength and ability to support ongoing investment.

Our Balanced Approach to AI Exposure

Our quality growth philosophy seeks to identify companies with sustainable business models, strong balance sheets, and solid financial returns - companies that we believe are well positioned to benefit from the long-term opportunities created by the evolution of AI.

Companies integrating AI to help strengthen existing franchises - by improving customer engagement, operational efficiency, or product innovation - fit more closely with our investment philosophy than those whose valuations rely primarily on AI-related narratives and speculation.

We believe our large-cap growth portfolio reflects a balanced approach to investing in companies with AI exposure. For example, in the software industry, the portfolio has exposure to established AI leaders such as Microsoft (MSFT), which is a key beneficiary of the ongoing buildout of AI infrastructure and enterprise adoption.

But at the same time, we own underappreciated software companies, including ServiceNow (NOW), Tyler Technologies (TYL), and Intuit (INTU). We believe these businesses have durable growth profiles and opportunities to integrate AI into their platforms over time, but on a year-to-date basis, they have lagged the market despite solid fundamentals and strong earnings growth.

We believe the companies mentioned above currently offer a more attractive risk/reward profile than a company such as Palantir Technologies (PLTR). While Palantir Technologies has developed compelling technology, the stock appears priced for perfection, trading at roughly 230x earnings and 100x sales - levels that leave little margin for execution risk.

Large-Cap Growth Software - Underappreciated Opportunities in Our Portfolio

While the market has favored direct AI beneficiaries, several high-quality software businesses have been overlooked. We see compelling opportunities among high-quality companies with strong fundamentals and durable growth whose stocks trade at far more attractive valuations than stocks such as Palantir Technologies.

Past performance is not indicative of future returns.

Conclusion

We believe AI represents a transformative and enduring theme, but one that will experience cycles of enthusiasm and consolidation along the way. As disciplined quality growth investors, we don’t attempt to time those cycles.

Instead, we focus on distinguishing between durable beneficiaries versus merely speculative participants in the market. Our approach remains firmly anchored in identifying structurally advantaged growth companies with strong fundamentals and durable long-term growth potential.

data.title: Astera Labs: Riding AI Sustains Growth Above Peers
data.description:
Introduction & Investment Thesis

With the AI data center evolution globally from siloed GPU clusters to unified, racked powerhouses, I find Astera Labs, Inc. (ALAB) to be emerging as a critical neural network which wires them together. The stock is trading at $164.32 (as of December 11, 2025), a 39% growth over the last year, beating the S&P 500’s 14.12% despite recent volatility.

Over the last couple of quarters, this company has demonstrated remarkable growth amid strong industry tailwinds, which I believe makes it a decent growth pick. For example, in Q3 2025, its revenue surged by about 104% YoY to $230.6 million, and its non-GAAP EPS came in at $0.49, a significant growth from Q3 2024 EPS of -$0.05. Most interestingly, its Aries retimers, Scorpio switches, and Leo CXL controllers are capturing hyperscalers' demand for PCle 6.0 and NVLink Fusion ecosystems. I see a massive macro tailwind, such as the more than $1 trillion AI deals. Given the company’s solid growth and catalysts, I rate this stock a buy for growth-oriented investors.

The Macro Lens: Market & Industry Context

One of the notable trends in the tech industry is the transformation of data centers from power hungry monoliths into efficient, disaggregated ecosystems. With connectivity at the center of this transformation, I will begin by looking at the market potential here. According to Mordor Intelligence, the global AI-in-IoT market is projected to grow by a CAGR of 22.68% between 2025 and 2030, from a market size of $60.71 billion to $168.69 billion.

This growth will be driven by edge AI, 5G latency cuts, and rack-scale demand for 10x bandwidth over PCle 5.0. Hyperscalers such as AWS, Google, and Microsoft are pouring more than $300 billion into CapEx in 2025,, prioritizing open standards like CXL 3.0 and UALink to counter Nvidia’s proprietary lock-in. Ethernet and PCle optical interconnects now dominate, with 200G+ speeds essential for training trillion-parameter models.

The current geopolitical shifts are making the market even more complex. For instance, the US-China tensions are spurring reshoring, thus boosting Astera’s TSMC reliance but risking tariffs (up to 10-15% on imports). Just for context, the tensions block Astera from using Chinese foundries like the SMIC, thus forcing deeper reliance on TSMC as the only compliant advanced node partner. This happens at a time when the CHIPS Act incentives and the surging AI demand are channeling more hyperscalers' volume to TSMC, thus securing Astera capacity. Either way, energy efficiency mandates AI racks consuming about 100kW favors Astera’s low-power DSP retimers. It is important to note that AI workloads demand massive bandwidth of up to 64GT/s per lane while operating under tight power envelopes. This demand comes alongside growing power consumption, which needs to be managed to achieve sustainability goals.

Astera retimers’ addresses this need by regenerating signals with minimal energy overhead enabling 3x reach extension. The most important aspect is their low-power design such as the 11W (2W lower than the closest rival) for a 16-lane PCle 6.x configuration. Allow me to give a specific example in the market of how Astera's unique features are positioning it competitively in the market. In NVidia’s next-generation GPU systems, such as the GB200 NVL72 racks, Aries 6 smart DSP retimers provides PCle 6.2/CXL 3.x connectivity between GPUs, CPUs, NICs and accelerators. In a demo at GTC 2024, Aries 6 achieved 64GT/s x16 links at 10-11W outperforming Gen5 retimers in power while doubling bandwidth. This shows how Astera can cut power consumption while increasing performance, which sets it apart in the industry.

When I look at the competitive landscape, I find that Broadcom (AVGO) and Marvell (MRVL) focus on the Ethernet switching silicon market. On the contrary, Astera’s focus on AI-specific connectivity differentiates it via its end-to-end platform (hardware+COSMOS software). In my view this explains its wins with NVidia GB300 and AMD’s MI300X. In summary, Astera is offering standout solutions that fit the growing needs for high performance AI chips at low-energy consumption. This explains why major AI giants such as NVDA and AMD are some of its key customers, and this reflects the company’s strong market position.

The Fundamentals Check: Explosive Growth, Higher Margins Outshining Peers

Upon examining this company’s financials, I find them screaming acceleration. For example, its trailing revenue stands at $723 million, a 136.51% YoY growth, way above AVGO’s 28.01% and MRVL’s 44.95%. From a quarterly perspective, its Q3 2025 revenue grew by 103.89% YoY, which was significantly higher than the two competitors’ average of 29.44%. Notably, the third quarter of 2025 marked the 8th successive quarter of triple-figure revenue growth rate, an indication of its strong revenue momentum and strong market position.

Moving to profitability, its trailing net income stands at $198.87 million, flipping from a 2024 net loss of $83.42 million and marking the end of at least a 3-year streak of net losses.

When I look at the margins, I find that ALAB has the highest gross margin and the lowest net margin. If I couple this phenomenon with its unmatched growth, I see a typical young disruptor against the incumbents. Interestingly, its net margin curve demonstrates a steep upward slope compared to the peers, a testament that it’s likely to catch up or even beat the peers given the company’s solid growth momentum.

If I were to sum these financials up, I would say ALAB is a classic example of growth at scale. It is converting AI tailwinds into explosive growth while preserving core profitability, unlike slower-growth companies burdened by high costs.

Given the company’s competitive market position anchored on its unique products, I am confident that this company will sustain its solid growth momentum. This aligns with the company’s guidance, which projects revenue of $245 million-$253 million, about 6%-10% above the Q3 levels and a continuation of the triple-digit growth trajectory. While I expect market saturation to kick in, which is with respect to the normal growth curve? I expect this company’s growth to fall within the double-digit range, which I think is also attractive. I believe any range between 20% and 50%, which is within range with the global AI market growth of 30.6% by 2033, is something sustainable in the long run, which is also reflected in the consensus estimates.

Valuation: Justified Premium Pricing

Considering this company's robust topline growth, I will value it using the P/S multiple compared to AVGO and MRVL. Of the three companies, ALAB has the highest P/S multiple at 37.28x, which shows that the company is trading at a premium relative to its peers.

However, upon looking at the trailing revenue growth rate, Astera’s 136.51% YoY growth is significantly higher than the peers’ average of 36.48%. This implies that ALAB’s growth is 3.74x the average of its two competitors. Looking at the P/S multiples, you realize that Astera’s valuation is just 1.75x the average of the competitors, meaning that although the premium valuation is justified, the market is still undervaluing ALAB’s growth. However, given that the current growth is likely to slow down with respect to the normal growth curve, I think the current valuation is to some extent reasonable. For this reason, I rate this growth stock a buy.

Risks

Customer concentration risk

This company draws about 80% of its total revenue from three customers, something that makes it vulnerable to shifts in their spending or architectural preferences. As such, any budget reallocation or delays in AI deployment could impact the company’s financial performance significantly, halting its growth momentum. This could in turn affect the stock performance given its volatility, as shown by its beta of 2.55, which is more than double the market’s beta of 1.

Conclusion

I reiterate my buy rating for this stock for growth-oriented investors given the massive market opportunity that the company is seizing through its innovative products. Its low-energy consumption products, which come with higher performance, suit the AI’s rack-scale future, something I believe will sustain its long-term growth. I recommend paying a close attention to the Q4 earnings report for updates on Scorpio X ramps and UALink progress because they will offer insights on the market performance of these products, which are key for its sustainable growth.

data.title: Invesco Global Core Equity Fund: Q3 2025 Portfolio Positioning And Performance Highlights
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from the Invesco Global Core Equity Fund Q3 2025 Commentary.

We added new positions in Coca-Cola Europacific Partners (CCEP), Contemporary Amperex Technology (CTATF) and WEC Energy (WEC). We sold Unilever (UL), IMCD (IMCDY) and Progressive (PGR).

Coca-Cola Europacific Partners is the sole distributor of Coca-Cola products in Europe, Australia, Indonesia and the Philippines. We see CCEP as an important element of the Coke ecosystem, connecting the brand owner to a highly fragmented customer base. In our view, the company has a very strong record of operational and financial management under the leadership of Damian Gammell. We funded this purchase with the sale of Unilever where we determined the turnaround we identified in early 2024 was now more fairly priced into the shares.

Contemporary Amperex Technology is the leading manufacturer of electric vehicle batteries. We believe the company is becoming increasingly dominant due to its low cost, vertically integrated operations and highly innovative product development. Electric vehicles remain in our view a strong growth opportunity, and we are also enthusiastic about the potential role the company's grid scale battery storage products can play in balancing national power demands. We sold IMCD to fund this purchase.

WEC Energy is a regulated utility operating primarily in Wisconsin. It has a record of generating stable financial returns and benefits from a steady regulatory regime. We expect an acceleration in the company's growth profile as Wisconsin appears set to benefit from the build-out of multiple new data center clusters.

Progressive was sold because we think the company could experience slowing growth as competitors have now recovered from historic underwriting losses. We believe Progressive remains a unique insurance business and we could see the fund owning it again provided we identify an attractive valuation opportunity.

Performance highlights

The fund underperformed its benchmark primarily due to holdings in the information technology and financials sectors, which lagged those of the benchmark during the quarter.

In contrast, the fund's holdings in the communication services and healthcare sectors outperformed those of the benchmark during the quarter and added to relative performance.

Contributors to performance

The largest contributors to relative return were Contemporary Amperex Technology, ASML (ASML) and Tencent.

Contemporary Amperex Technology was a recent purchase that appreciated strongly shortly after we added it to the fund. We discuss our view of the company in more detail under Portfolio Positioning.

ASML, a company specializing in photolithography machines for the semiconductor industry, recovered as the equity market seemingly has begun to price in a recovery in order trends after two years of muted growth.

Tencent delivered another set of strong financial results. The company remains highly embedded in the day-to-day lives of Chinese consumer through its ubiquitous WeChat app and appears to be benefiting significantly from the application of AI inside its advertising business.

Detractors from performance

The largest detractors from relative return were Constellation Software, Tesla (TSLA) and Canadian Pacific Kansas City (CP:CA).

Constellation Software reported good financial results, but shares fell as concerns about potential for AI to disrupt its business appeared to mount. Mark Leonard, the company's founder, also announced his retirement as CEO, for health reasons. We reduced the fund's position in this stock.

Tesla shares recovered as Elon Musk returned to full time management of the company, undoubtedly a positive for Tesla owners, and bought $1 billion of stock in the open market. The fund does not own Tesla because we still have reservations about increased competitive intensity within its core automotive business.

Canadian Pacific Kansas City , a Canada-based railway, underperformed in response to tepid volume trends and concerns about the impact of tariffs on Mexico and Canada. The announced merger of Union Pacific (UNP) (UNP) and Norfolk Southern (NSC) (NSC) is also perceived to present an increased competitive threat to Canadian Pacific.

About Risk

ESG is not a uniformly defined characteristic and as a result, information used by the Fund to evaluate such factors may not be readily available, complete or accurate, and may vary across providers and issuers. There is no guarantee that ESG considerations will enhance Fund performance.

data.title: Invesco Global Core Equity Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Key takeaways

Market leadership

A relatively narrow group of stocks seen to be AI beneficiaries continued to drive equity results. These performance leaders include the recipients of vast capital being invested to build out data center facilities and associated infrastructure.

Technology leaders

We believe Microsoft, Amazon, Meta and Google are relatively well-positioned to benefit from AI proliferation. We have seen clear evidence these businesses have been benefiting from it, though we note that significantly accelerating capital spending has been diminishing their free cash flow generation.

Portfolio positioning

We believe the fund is relatively diverse across a variety of consumer, industrial, financial and healthcare companies. We focus on businesses we believe have strong and enduring sources of competitive advantage and talented and aligned managers, with a prudent approach to balance sheet risk.

Manager perspective and outlook

  • Global equities delivered positive returns in the third quarter of 2025, supported by sustained enthusiasm toward artificial intelligence (AI), central bank interest rate cuts and robust corporate earnings. In this environment, emerging market equities outperformed their developed market counterparts.
  • Emerging market outperformance largely stemmed from Chinese equities, which benefited from easing of US trade tensions, growing optimism about AI-related developments and a weaker US dollar.
  • Among developed markets, US equities posted solid gains, bolstered by strong second quarter earnings. The US Federal Reserve's first rate cut of the year, along with signals of further easing, also supported US stocks.
  • Japanese equities advanced, aided by a new US trade agreement that reduced tariffs, ongoing corporate governance reforms and a resilient domestic economy.
  • In contrast, German equities declined during the quarter, weighed down by a stagnating economy, weak manufacturing and exports, rising US trade tensions and heightened political uncertainty.

Portfolio positioning

We added new positions in Coca-Cola Europacific Partners (CCEP), Contemporary Amperex Technology (CTATF) and WEC Energy (WEC). We sold Unilever (UL), IMCD (IMCDY) and Progressive (PGR).

Coca-Cola Europacific Partners (CCEP) is the sole distributor of Coca-Cola products in Europe, Australia, Indonesia and the Philippines. We see CCEP as an important element of the Coke ecosystem, connecting the brand owner to a highly fragmented customer base. In our view, the company has a very strong record of operational and financial management under the leadership of Damian Gammell. We funded this purchase with the sale of Unilever where we determined the turnaround we identified in early 2024 was now more fairly priced into the shares.

Contemporary Amperex Technology is the leading manufacturer of electric vehicle batteries. We believe the company is becoming increasingly dominant due to its low cost, vertically integrated operations and highly innovative product development. Electric vehicles remain in our view a strong growth opportunity, and we are also enthusiastic about the potential role the company's grid scale battery storage products can play in balancing national power demands. We sold IMCD to fund this purchase.

WEC Energy is a regulated utility operating primarily in Wisconsin. It has a record of generating stable financial returns and benefits from a steady regulatory regime. We expect an acceleration in the company's growth profile as Wisconsin appears set to benefit from the build-out of multiple new data center clusters.

Progressive was sold because we think the company could experience slowing growth as competitors have now recovered from historic underwriting losses. We believe Progressive remains a unique insurance business and we could see the fund owning it again provided we identify an attractive valuation opportunity.

The fund underperformed its benchmark primarily due to holdings in the information technology and financials sectors, which lagged those of the benchmark during the quarter.

Contributors to performance

The largest contributors to relative return were Contemporary Amperex Technology, ASML (ASML) and Tencent.

Contemporary Amperex Technology was a recent purchase that appreciated strongly shortly after we added it to the fund. We discuss our view of the company in more detail under Portfolio Positioning.

ASML , a company specializing in photolithography machines for the semiconductor industry, recovered as the equity market seemingly has begun to price in a recovery in order trends after two years of muted growth.

Tencent delivered another set of strong financial results. The company remains highly embedded in the day-to-day lives of Chinese consumer through its ubiquitous WeChat app

Tesla shares recovered as Elon Musk returned to full time management of the company, undoubtedly a positive for Tesla owners, and bought $1 billion of stock in the open market.
