data.title: TFLO: When In Doubt, Cash Is The Answer
data.description:
Thesis

The iShares Treasury Floating Rate Bond ETF (TFLO) is a fixed income fund that we last covered in 2024 and that we own. The name comes from iShares and represents an ETF geared towards floating-rate treasuries.

In today's article we are going to discuss the latest move by the Federal Reserve, the uncertainties present in the market, and the ability for a fund like TFLO to represent a hedge.

Firstly, let us revisit what TFLO actually does

The fund invests in a niche asset class, namely floating-rate treasuries. Floating rate notes ('FRNs') are a sector of the treasury market, and they represent government obligations that have a frequent reset feature. Unlike T-Bills which trade at a discount, FRNs trade like regular securities, but their reset feature gives them a very low duration profile. They thus straddle T-Bills and T-bonds.

Rates have been cut again

On December 10, 2025 the Fed cut rates again to a range of between 3.5% and 3.75%, the lowest in more than three years. This simply means that effective Fed Funds will end up in that range and that all money market instruments will see lower yields going forward. Fed Funds is the crux of monetary policy, and while it works with a lag, it does end up dragging down the front end of the yield curve.

TFLO will soon show lower yields and will adjust its dividend lower to match the new levels in the market. The fund does what it is supposed to do and will always follow the very front end of the yield curve.

It is interesting to note, though, that the terminal neutral rate as per SOFR futures has not really changed:

SOFR futures still show a terminal rate at 3%, despite the expected changes in the Fed chair next year. That should put today's levels fairly close to that terminal level.

The equity market is very extended. So is credit.

The issue with today's markets is overvaluation in many corners. By traditional metrics, we are witnessing stretched markets in both equities and credit. Let us take equities, for example:

By historic P/E metrics, equities are currently stretched. In the above chart, courtesy of Goldman, the blue squares represent the historic P/E ranges, while the orange dot represents the current P/E figure. As we can see in the U.S., that number is very high above the normal range. Can equities continue to remain high or power even higher? You bet. Is it rational to buy them here? We think not. In fact, we are of the opinion that savvy investors can turn more conservative by selling some equities exposure and buying a fund like TFLO. One does not need to time a market to perfection but just recognize signs of over- and undervaluation and adjust a portfolio as such.

Looking at credit (U.S. high yield, to be more specific), we get a similar picture:

If we look at the ICE BofA BB US High Yield Index OAS, we can notice it is at the bottom of its historic range at a 1.73% level. We liked spreads in 2023 when they were above 3%. The bottom of the range is not a place to buy but to sell (when it comes to spreads, which have an inverse relationship to price). Is credit stretched as well? History says yes.

While some people will advocate for just holding equities or credit for very long periods of time, common sense says that buying overvalued assets will result in short term pain. In our view, selling some overvalued assets to keep more cash is not a bad idea at all.

Cash is the cheapest way to hedge

There are many ways to hedge a portfolio, and some investors think that they can time the market and make money even during a recession. It is much harder than it sounds. Traditional 'hedging' involves buying puts or inverse ETFs, instruments that can cost significant capital. Both those options also involve market timing.

If puts are bought but the horizon is not long enough, an investor loses the option premium. If inverse funds are bought and the market rallies, the downside is technically infinite, but what usually ends up happening is a closing of the position at a loss. 'Hedging' without market timing is always a cash burning proposition.

Sitting in cash is a very tame way to hedge, but a very efficient endeavor. If you want less risk, just put more capital in cash (we consider TFLO a cash equivalent). The worst that can happen is that you earn money market rates. There is no option premium to decay, and there is no inverse ETF momentum to worry about. After trying at many instances to time the market, we have learned that it usually ends up being a money-losing endeavor. That is why we simply increase our TFLO balance for 'hedging' purposes.

Conclusion

TFLO is a fixed-income ETF. The name invests in floating-rate treasuries, thus capturing the front end of the yield curve. The fund has a very low duration and no credit risk, thus representing a 'cash-like' instrument. We own this fund and consider it a portfolio building block. Markets are very stretched by traditional valuation metrics currently, and we consider cash to be a safe way to de-risk a portfolio. Buying puts or inverse ETFs involves market timing, a very difficult feat to achieve.

data.title: Rubrik: Strong Fundamentals, Why Now Is The Time To Buy
data.description:
Rubrik (NYSE:RBRK) is a cybersecurity company in a category that some may not have heard of: Cyber Resilience. In my last article about this company, published on October 18, 2025, I explained why it is misunderstood and provided a detailed overview of what it does. I also rated the stock a buy for aggressive growth investors. That advice didn't look so hot at first. The stock sank from $78.66 at the article's publication date to an intraday low of $67.24 on November 20, a 14.5% decline.

However, the stock recovered to $89.10 by December 8, 2025, mainly on the back of excellent third-quarter fiscal year ("FY") 2026 earnings. The stock rose 22.5% on December 5 to $86.27 in response to the earnings report.

Investors were pleased to see solid positive revenue growth; third-quarter FY 2026 revenue increased by 48% year over year to $350.2 million, beating analysts' estimates by $29.66 million. More importantly, several indicators pointed to robust revenue growth continuing.

Rubrik uses a software-as-a-service (SaaS) business model, one of the market's most highly valued growth strategies, because it generates recurring revenue that is highly predictable and stable. When we get to the valuation section of this article, it will show that some valuation interpretations consider Rubrik overvalued. However, one reason growth investors will allow the stock to trade at a premium valuation is its stable, predictable revenue.

Growth investors measure the health of its SaaS business by its subscription Annual Recurring Revenue ("ARR"). SaaS investors also consider ARR as high-quality revenue because it can generate high-margin revenue over time. In a subscription model, the incremental cost of serving existing customers often declines over time, allowing profits to grow faster than revenue, which is the definition of operating leverage. As a result, early-stage SaaS business models have a strong potential to scale into profitability if they grow ARR rapidly and efficiently. Generally, the faster a SaaS company grows ARR, the more willing the market is to accept higher valuations.

Rubrik grew its third-quarter FY 2026 ARR by 34% year over year.

Despite strong ARR and revenue growth, some investors don't want to invest because they're confused about what this company does. In my first article on Rubrik, I stated, "Some investors still view it [Rubrik] as a "backup and data recovery" platform." One reason that perception exists is that Rubrik launched its first product in 2015 as an on-premise, self-managed backup and recovery platform. However, the company has expanded significantly beyond backup and recovery since 2015.

It has also moved beyond its initial focus on on-prem products, first selling SaaS cloud applications and later offering a fully managed, cloud-native SaaS platform named Rubrik Security Cloud ("RSC"), which it released in 2022. This data security platform combines data protection (backup/recovery) with advanced security features, including ransomware detection, sensitive data discovery, and cyber recovery.

The company doesn't force its customers to use its cloud products; it maintains support for on-prem deployments for customers who have not yet transitioned. However, it highly encourages them to transition to the cloud.

Investors can measure the company's success in transitioning customers to the cloud by tracking Cloud ARR growth and the proportion of Cloud ARR within overall Subscription ARR.

The main reason investors take interest in Cloud ARR is that the company can grow cloud revenue faster than on-prem revenue, without a proportional rise in costs, resulting in higher-margin revenue. In other words, the company can achieve profitability faster by increasing Cloud ARR to a higher share of Subscription ARR.

Cloud ARR also measures the company's progress in selling its RSC. Chief Financial Officer ("CFO") Kiran Choudary stated on the third quarter FY 2026 earnings call, "We continue to drive adoption of our Rubrik Security Cloud, which resulted in $1.17 billion of cloud ARR up 53%."

Another critical metric for a SaaS company is net retention ratio (NRR). Rubrik's version of NRR is Average Subscription Dollar-Based NRR, which represents the percentage of revenue retained and expanded from existing subscribers over the past year. It includes upsells and expansions, minus downgrades and churn. Although gaining new customer revenue is essential, growing revenue from existing subscribers is important for a SaaS company to become a sustainable operation, and NRR is one measure of that sustainability.

Rubrik's third quarter FY 2026 Average Subscription Dollar-Based NRR was above 120%, which is among the best in the SaaS industry. An NRR above 120% combined with excellent gross margins, growing free cash flow ("FCF"), and operating leverage is often the recipe for the market awarding a stock a premium valuation.

Another metric that indicates a company showing robust revenue growth and maintaining solid SaaS metrics is the growth in the number of customers contributing $100,000 or more in Subscription ARR, as it reflects growth among large customers. Large subscription customers are more likely to spend more over time and remain loyal to the company than smaller ones, which tend to churn more often. Larger companies help maintain NRR above 120% and make it easier to maintain ARR growth because they produce more bang for the buck. Investors frequently view this metric as evidence that the company can execute, penetrate large accounts, and get larger customers to spend on its data security, backup, and Artificial Intelligence ("AI") solutions. Rubrik increased the number of customers contributing $100,000 or more in subscription ARR in the third quarter by 27% year over year to 2,638.

The market also likes that the increased topline growth is translating to expanding gross margins and an expanding FCF margin, as it is evidence that the company is on the way to operational sustainability. The following chart shows non-GAAP (Generally Accepted Accounting Principles) gross margin expanded by 5 points year over year in the third quarter of FY 2026, and non-GAAP FCF expanded from negative 5% to positive 18% during the same period.

The big question is: after the recent price surge, should investors continue to invest in the stock at its current valuation? Some analysts believe so. The consensus opinion was generally favorable toward third-quarter results. The Wall Street analyst community maintained a strong-buy consensus rating, with an average price target of $115.05 through December 9.

Management highlighted several ways the company could maintain solid revenue growth beyond the core data protection business in the future, including expansion into agentic AI, a hot investment theme for investors these days, for good reason. It is one of the fastest-growing areas in technology and a prime area for cybersecurity investment. Grand View Research estimated the global agentic security market was only $22.56 billion at the end of 2024. It forecasts that the demand for those services will grow at a compound annual growth rate ("CAGR") of 34.4% from 2025 to 2033, reaching $322.39 billion. If you invest in Rubrik, you are counting on the company continuing to execute and to capture significant market share in new areas, such as the rapidly expanding agentic security market, which would go a long way toward helping the company maintain Subscription ARR growth, drive revenue growth, and eventually achieve profitability.

The launch of Rubrik Agent Cloud

The company released Rubrik Agent Cloud ("RAC") as a beta product for a select few customers on October 22, 2025. Investors are highly interested in this product's rollout, as it represents a new and possibly massive revenue stream from an area that thematic investors see as the next significant growth wave beyond generative Artificial Intelligence ("AI"): Agentic AI.

Rubrik built RAC around a company it had been pursuing for around nine months, called Predibase. The company surprised the market by doing the opposite of what most security companies do. Chief Executive Officer ("CEO") Bipul Sinha said at the Barclays 23rd Annual Global Technology Conference:

We started to think about what is a great middleware solution in AI that can change the game for us. And we came across this company called Predibase, which they were doing model fine-tuning and GPU virtualization to reduce the cost of inference by multiplexing different models on the same GPU. And we saw that company and say, "Wallah, I mean, this is really a very interesting long-term strategy for us." And the whole market was confused that why are we buying a model infrastructure company.

The average Cybersecurity company's standard acquisitions incorporate AI for specific cybersecurity products, such as endpoint detection, identity protection, threat intelligence, or Zero Trust, which they can easily bolt onto an existing cybersecurity platform.

Before Rubrik purchased it, Predibase was an AI infrastructure company that focused on maximizing the processing power of Graphics Processing Units ("GPUs") to speed up the training and inference of AI models, thereby improving their efficiency. Rubrik purchased Predibase, not as a bolt-on acquisition, but to acquire top AI expertise and build a speedy, efficient infrastructure for Rubrik Agent Cloud, designed explicitly to secure AI infrastructure and AI operations. In other words, instead of applying AI to security operations, as many security companies do, it's applying security to AI operations. Potential cybersecurity competitors without Predibase's AI infrastructure expertise will likely struggle to recreate similar AI security products, giving it a solid, defensible moat.

It announced the acquisition of Predibase in June 2025 and, upon closing the acquisition in August 2025, introduced a new product, Agent Rewind, based on the acquired technology and expertise. Agent Rewind enables companies to observe and record their AI agents' actions, and rewind any undesirable actions, such as deleting essential information or sending incorrect messages.

CEO Bipul Sinha discussed Agent Rewind at the Barclays 23rd Annual Global Technology Conference:

So we created this whole framework about creating guardrail for agentic work. And Predibase had model fine-tuning. So if the agents are not responding with the right answer, you can actually increase the accuracy by taking data. Again, Rubrik has the data to fine-tune the model. And then finally, agent could still misbehave because somebody sitting in North Korea could compromise your agent and run your customer refund process million times and make you bankrupt. How do you find out what is happening and undo those actions? So we provided a rewind button. So our view is that for agentic work in the enterprise, you need actually an infrastructure platform that sits at the intersection of security and operations because for every call that agents are making, you need to dynamically authorize whether it's allowed or not. And so we built this whole end-to-end platform. So we have a very big long-term vision, very, very early days for us. We are still in the beta. Our first release is not too far in the future.

In my last article on the company, I stated, "The company's [Rubrik's] TAM should be $41.02 billion at the end of calendar year 2025." The company also estimated in its S-1 that its Total addressable Market ("TAM") would be "$52.9 billion by the end of calendar year 2027." According to the earlier TAM I cited from Grand View Research, the TAM for Agentic Security should reach $30.91 billion by the end of 2025, growing at a 34.4% CAGR from 2025 to 2033. So, RAC should add significant revenue growth potential to Rubrik.

If Rubrik successfully cross-sells RAC to existing customers and sells to new ones, ARR and NRR could increase. Investors would likely assign the stock a higher valuation based on those improved SaaS metrics.

Since the market has likely not yet priced in the potential revenue upside from RAC into the valuation, investors may undervalue the stock, despite some traditional valuation indicators suggesting overvaluation. Once the market starts recognizing revenue from RAC, the stock's valuation could rise quickly.

Risks

The most significant reason some investors steer clear of this stock is that it is still GAAP (Generally Accepted Accounting Principles) unprofitable. Although the company is already FCF positive, more conservative long-term investors are likely far from satisfied because, to demonstrate long-term sustainability, it must achieve consistent GAAP operating profitability. There will always be a meaningful portion of the market that discounts the stock's valuation and considers it overvalued as long as it displays a lack of operating profitability, with good reason. As long as it remains unprofitable, there is a risk that something could happen to prevent or delay the company from becoming profitable and self-sustaining.

However, bullish investors may argue that the company has operating leverage, which reduces the risk that it will never achieve profitability.

A few years ago, I came across the term contribution margin and was confused about what it was and why it was important. I researched and discovered that it measures a company's operating leverage, or its ability to increase profits faster than revenue growth. When you subtract a company's variable costs from its revenue, the amount left over is the contribution dollars, which the company can use to pay overhead, including fixed costs such as rent, insurance premiums, property taxes, and payroll. Once a company covers its fixed costs, accountants classify the remaining amount as earnings. Contribution dollars divided by revenue equals the contribution margin.

Rubrik uses a version of the contribution margin called the non-GAAP Subscription ARR contribution margin. This metric measures how much its initiative to transition customers to the cloud improves Rubrik's gross margins and reduces Research and Development (R&D) and Sales & Marketing (S&M) costs. When this metric improves, it signals the company is on the road to operating profitability. Rubrik's third-quarter FY 2026 trailing 12-month ("TTM") or Last 12-months ("LTM") non-GAAP Subscription ARR contribution margin is 10%, indicating that the company's core SaaS business is on the road to operating profitability, which vastly improves investor sentiment toward the company. The stock's dramatic rise post-earnings is not only due to solid topline growth but also to progress in increasing the contribution margin.

However, the company has other issues that some investors may perceive as red flags. Conservative investors may have problems with its balance sheet.

At the end of its third quarter FY 2026, Rubrik had $1.603 billion in cash and $1.130 billion in long-term debt.

However, the company has a negative debt-to-equity (D/E) ratio of -2.22. Its negative D/E reflects a negative equity of $524.4 million, which may cause some investors to give this one a wide berth. Investors hate negative equity because it can signal potential financial distress.

A negative D/E may also indicate that the company has little financial flexibility; Rubrik may find it challenging to sell any of its assets, refinance a loan, or borrow cash against its equity if the economy tanks or the Information Technology ("IT") goes into a downturn.

However, a negative D/E can also indicate that the company has accumulated significant past losses or incurred substantial stock-based compensation. In Rubrik's case, it's both.

Anyone considering this stock should be aware that its stock-based compensation-to-revenue ratio is 23.55%, indicating potential future dilution for existing investors. A 23.55% ratio is relatively high and may disqualify Rubrik from investment by some investors.

The following chart shows the company has accumulated significant GAAP net losses across 2024 and has begun to move toward GAAP net income in 2025.

One of the biggest reasons the market wants the company to become GAAP profitable is that it can reverse negative accumulated earnings and eventually make them positive, putting the company on a more sustainable path with positive equity.

The company recorded a net loss per share of 0.32 for the third quarter of FY 2026, beating analysts' estimates by $0.21. Pay close attention to whether the company can exceed analysts' future earnings expectations, as both net income and dilution affect earnings. If the company misses earnings expectations, be aware that it may offset positive sentiment from strong revenue growth, and the stock may decline.

The company has a debt-to-EBITDA (Earnings Before Interest, Taxes, Depreciation, and Amortization) ratio of -3.27, reflective of its negative EBITDA. Conservative investors often avoid such companies because a company with negative EBITDA makes it nearly impossible to gauge its ability to repay debt. Generally, the market only trusts the company's ability to repay loans because of its robust revenue generation and growing FCF. If the company ever misses revenue estimates, the stock will likely sink like a rock. Investors should also monitor the company's FCF margin and FCF. If its FCF ever shows a substantial decline, the market may punish the stock.

Rubrik bulls might argue that the company is shielded from immediate bankruptcy risk by its $1.60 billion cash balance. The economy, or its business, would need to enter a severe downturn before it goes into financial distress.

The market may have worried more about its negative EBITDA if Rubrik had not already achieved positive FCF. Although the market uses EBITDA to judge a company's ability to repay debt, companies actually use FCF to service debt.

Valuation

Rubrik's TTM price-to-sales (P/S) ratio is 13.89, well above the IT median of 3.49, indicating potential overvaluation. Even when the stock hit a P/S ratio of 10.80 on November 20, some conservative value investors likely still considered it overvalued.

The following chart compares Rubrik's TTM P/S ratio and quarterly year-over-year revenue growth with those of several partial or direct competitors. YCharts is missing Broadcom's (AVGO) revenue growth. However, according to Broadcom's third-quarter FY 2025 earnings release, it's 22%.

Rubrik's TTM P/S ratio of 13.89 is higher than the median P/S ratio (9.52) of the companies that I compare it to on the chart above. I exclude Rubrik from my median calculation to avoid it influencing the median I am comparing it against. Rubrik's P/S ratio also trades above that of its most similar and direct competitor, Commvault Systems (CVLT) (5.009), an additional sign that conservative investors may use to call the stock overvalued.

However, some may interpret Rubrik's faster revenue growth rate over Commvault Systems, Okta (OKTA), Datadog (DDOG), and NetApp (NTAP) as justifying its higher TTM P/S ratio and may consider Rubrik deserving of its premium valuation compared to those companies.

Although Rubrik achieved the highest year-over-year quarterly revenue growth rate among its peers, Broadcom and Datadog have higher P/S ratios. If investors compare only revenue growth rates, some might consider Rubrik undervalued relative to Broadcom and Zscaler (ZS). As a result, growth investors may view Rubrik as deserving its premium valuation and as the best value among its peers, given its superior revenue growth. If you believe Rubrik deserves its premium valuation, your investment thesis relies heavily on the company's continued revenue growth. If the company misses revenue estimates or provides disappointing revenue guidance, the stock's decline could be steep.

The weakness of the above comparison is that it fails to measure the quality of revenue. For instance, Broadcom may deserve a premium valuation over Rubrik, even though Rubrik is growing revenue faster, if Broadcom has better gross margins or free cash flow ("FCF").

The following compares the same companies' two-year forward P/S ratios, estimated forward revenue growth rates, TTM gross margins, operating margins, TTM FCF margins, and TTM return on invested capital ("ROIC"). The following analysis accounts for revenue quality (margins).

Company Name Two-year Forward P/S ratio Two-Year Forward estimated revenue growth Two-year forward P/S ratio-to-estimated revenue growth (P/S/G) TTM FCF margin TTM Gross Margin TTM Operating Margin TTM ROIC
Rubrik 10.79 23.23% 0.46 20.30% 79.25 -31.69 -28.76%
Commvault Systems 4.39 12.45% 0.35 17.08% 80.61% 7.84 5.63%
Broadcom 21.41 35.70% 0.60 41.59% 63.13% 39.33% 12.07%
Zscaler 9.80 19.79% 0.49 29.91% 76.55% -4.14% -3.61%
Datadog 12.96 20.87% 0.62 26.92% 79.91% -1.38% -0.96%
Okta 4.79 9.22% 0.52 31.51% 77.08% 4.30% 0.95%
NetApp 3.28 4.90% 0.67 25.24% 70.06% 22.62% 16.21%

One of the best ways to compare these companies is on a two-year forward P/S/G basis. The market generally considers the company with the lowest P/S/G as undervalued relative to those with higher P/S/Gs.

As a result, some may consider Rubrik undervalued because its P/S/G of 0.46 is below the group median of 0.56. However, others may view it as overvalued relative to Commvault (0.35), the most similar company to Rubrik (0.46).

If you factor in TTM profitability metrics, conservative and value-oriented investors may penalize Rubrik for the worst operating margins (-31.69%) and ROIC (-28.76%) in the comparison and consider the company overvalued relative to the rest.

Currently, Rubrik lacks a TTM price-to-earnings (P/E) ratio. So, let's look at the company's forward-looking P/E and analysts' estimated earnings per share ("EPS") growth ("PEG") ratio. I will use FY 2029 numbers, as FY 2026, FY 2027, and FY 2028 contain either negative figures or astronomical numbers reflecting the company's earnings growth from a low base number.

Rubrik's FY 2029 PEG ratio is 1.06, calculated by dividing the company's FY 2029 forward P/E of 78.50 by analysts' estimated EPS growth of 73.94%. The market's general rule of thumb is that a PEG ratio of 1.0 indicates fair value. Growth investors generally consider a stock overvalued when its PEG ratio reaches 2.0.

Today, both value investors and growth investors would consider the stock's FY 2029 forward PEG of 1.06 as a sign of fair value. However, value investors and growth investors may disagree on whether the market overvalues the stock moving forward: value investors would likely classify it as overvalued if the PEG ratio rises further. In contrast, growth investors would consider it fairly valued until the PEG ratio goes above 2.0. In practical terms, growth investors might believe the stock has room to run, while value investors may believe it currently has little upside.

If the stock sold at a FY 2029 PEG ratio of 2.0, its price would be $168.58, a 84.40% rise over Rubrik's intraday price on December 9 of $91.42.

Like all valuation methods, this one has flaws. The biggest flaw in this PEG assessment is its reliance on only two analysts' estimates, a little over three years out. Generally, the market prices in forecasts only a year or a year and a half out because anything longer tends to be inaccurate. Only two analysts are willing to forecast out to FY 2029, indicating that the analyst community as a whole likely doesn't believe in the accuracy of making estimates for this stock that far out. It presupposes that the company will become profitable on the forecasted timeline, with no room for anything going wrong. So, take a FY 2029 PEG analysis with a massive grain of salt.

Whether you consider this stock overvalued depends a lot on your investment style. If you are a more conservative investor with a valuation bent, you may dislike the stock's relatively high TTM P/S. If you are a growth investor, you may believe the market undervalues its revenue growth rate.

Rubrik remains a buy

Conservative and value investors may want to avoid the stock, as a failure to meet analysts' estimates will likely lead to a rapid price decline.

However, up until now, the company has proven it can consistently execute and once again delivered a beat-and-raise quarter in the third quarter. The Chief Executive Officer ("CEO"), Bipul Sinha, noted the consistency of the company's performance on the third quarter earnings call:

Thank you to you, our shareholders, for your continued support and trust. A number of you have mentioned to me the consistency with which we have been delivering the beat and raise cadence since our IPO. This is true. We have done so quarter after quarter. We are confident in closing out this year strong and continuing our great momentum into the next year and beyond.

Until the company fails to execute, the wise bet for aggressive growth investors is that Rubrik will continue to exceed its guidance and analysts' expectations.

Management raised Rubrik's full-year FY 2026 subscription revenue from a range of $1.408 billion to $1.416 billion in the second quarter to a range of $1.439 billion to $1.453 billion, implying a year-over-year FY 2026 growth rate of approximately 32% at the midpoint of $1.446 billion. Management also increased the company's FY 2026 revenue guidance range from $1.227 billion to $1.237 billion in the second quarter to $1.280 billion to $1.282 billion in the third quarter, implying 54.5% year-over-year growth at the midpoint. If the company continues to execute and exceeds those estimates, it will generate a revenue growth well above its peers. With the narrative of future revenue growth from RAC and Agentic AI security on the table, the stock would likely see multiple expansion.

Management forecasts Subscription ARR Contribution margin of 9% for the fourth quarter and for the full FY 2026. If Rubrik meets this forecast, the market may begin pricing in a path to profitability. The market is also looking at its FCF. If the company exceeds its FCF guidance range, it will reduce balance-sheet risk and encourage investors to price the stock at higher valuations.

It may be challenging to forecast this company's long-term prospects because, although I didn't delve into this in this article, the company faces significant long-term competition. However, because I am reasonably sure this company will perform well in the short term (over the next year), I recommend buying it for risk-tolerant, aggressive growth investors.

data.title: Cellectis' Allogeneic CAR-T Potential Still Justifies A 'Buy'
data.description:
Cellectis S.A. (CLLS) is a biotech that leverages its Transcription Activator-Like Effector Nucleases (TALEN) and PulseAgile electroporation to develop allogeneic CAR-T medications. This way, CLLS targets conditions with high-unmet needs or heavily pretreated patients in B-cell malignancies. The company has two wholly owned lead assets. These are lasme-cel (UCART22) for r/r B-ALL in Phase 1/2, and eti-cel (UCART20x22) researched in Phase 1 for r/r B-cell NHL. CLLS also partnered with Allogene (ALLO), Servier, and Iovance (IOVA) to license its TALEN technology for r/r LBCL, r/r RCC, and melanoma. In my view, CLLS remains a great speculative "Buy" in this sector, which is why I reiterate my bullish stance on the stock.

CAR-T Candidates

Cellectis S.A. is a clinical-stage biotech working on gene editing and off-the-shelf CAR-T cell candidates for hematologic malignancies. CLLS was founded back in 1999 and is headquartered in Paris, France, with additional offices in New York City and Raleigh, North Carolina. Their facility in Paris (14,000 sq ft) is focused on CMC development, materials, and overall supporting CLLS' European operations. As for CLLS's Raleigh facilities (82,000 sq ft), these are dedicated to its US UCART GMP manufacturing, supply chain, and logistics. I initially covered CLLS in August 2024, and since then, the stock has appreciated by 118.8%, so I thought it was worth updating my thesis.

As for a quick recap, CLLS uses a gene-editing platform called Transcription Activator-Like Effector Nucleases (TALEN). This is an editing engine to turn normal donor cells into therapy cells. The platform is capable of making precise, repeatable edits in the same locations in every batch of cells to produce off-the-shelf cells in a standardized and controlled way. TALENs are engineered proteins that can recognize a specific DNA sequence and include a nuclease that cuts the DNA at that exact location. This way, CLLS's technology can replace, correct, or insert a gene in the cells.

Moreover, CLLS' TALE-based base editors can change one DNA base to another without creating a double-strand break. This is vital because double-strand breaks can cause genotoxicity, as it sometimes occurs with CRISPR nucleases. Additionally, TALE-based gene modulators can activate and inactivate genes without changing their DNA sequence. You see, TALENs are capable of: 1) removing the T-cell receptor, so donor T-Cells don't attack a patient, which reduces graft-versus-host disease risks. TALENs also 2) inactivating immune markers and help cells survive certain lymphodepleting drugs. Lastly, TALENs 3) inserting new genes (like CARs) that help T-Cells recognize and kill cancer cells.

CLLS also leverages a PulseAgile electroporation to introduce its editing tools into cells. PulseAgile works at a manufacturing scale, and it's designed for delivering CLLS's tools efficiently while keeping the cells viable. CLLS's allogeneic approach starts with donor T-Cells from healthy individuals. Then, CLLS uses TALEN and PulseAgile to remove parts that would cause rejection or graft-versus-host disease, and insert a CAR or other useful modifications. The company expands and freezes large batches of edited cells as doses that can be used for several patients, which is akin to storing biologic vials. Interestingly, this manufacturing process is also very scalable because a single batch can produce hundreds of doses. The final product is a pre-made off-the-shelf compound that works as a custom autologous cell product.

It's also worth mentioning that CLLS has three candidates in partnered programs that use its technology but are developed by other companies. First, 1) Cema-cel (ALLO-501A) is an allogeneic CD19-targeted CAR-T cell built on the TALEN gene-editing platform, which is in Phase 2 for r/r LBCL. Second, 2) Allo-316 is an allogeneic CAR-T that targets CD70 for r/r renal cell carcinoma (RCC) in Phase 1. And third, 3) IOV-4001 is an allogeneic, gene-edited tumor-infiltrating lymphocyte (TIL) product for melanoma in Phase 1.

Allogeneic Pipeline

With that in mind, CLLS's pipeline includes two fully-owned candidates called 1) lasme-cel (or UCART22), and eti-cel (or UCART20x22). UCART22 is in a Phase 1/2 trial (BALLI-01). BALLI-01 enrolled 40 young adults who have relapsed multiple times. The first phase of BALLI-01 used an earlier product made by a contract manufacturer (CDMO). But its second leg is using an improved P2 product that's manufactured by CLLS in its own facility, at different dose levels. BALLI-01's P1 product was initially administered to 18 patients and had a 28% ORR.

Additionally, UCART22 is a CD22-targeted CAR-T cell candidate for relapsed or refractory B-cell acute lymphoblastic leukemia (r/r B-ALL). Note that patients treated with UCART20x22 typically haven't responded to multiple prior treatments like blinatumomab, inotuzumab, and CD19 CAR-T. And interestingly, P2 was dosed to 22 patients and reached a much higher 68% ORR with an impressive 36% complete remission (CR/CRi) rate. And the highest dose (for 12 patients) got a 83% ORR and 42% with CR/CRi. Currently, CLLS anticipates its first pivotal Phase 2 readout by 2026, which I believe will be a positive catalyst for the stock as well, based on the earlier promising data.

As for UCART20x22, this allogeneic dual-target CAR-T focuses on CD20 and CD22 for r/r B-cell non-Hodgkin lymphoma (r/r NHL). UCART20x22 is in Phase 1 trials (NATHALI-01) with a cohort of 7 patients (18-80 years). These patients have at least two prior lines of therapy, including CD19 CAR-T. The trial is designed to find the best dose and then expand at that dose with large B-cell lymphoma (LBCL) patients. Up to now, its results have been positive with an ORR of 86% and a CR of 57%. And by 2026, we should get UCART20x22's End-of-Phase-1 (EoP1) data, which CLLS intends to use to discuss a follow-up Phase 2 design with the FDA.

Valuation And Risk Analysis

Now, from a valuation perspective, CLLS still trades at a relatively tiny market cap of $362.4 million despite having several promising value verticals. Their latest press release for Q3 2025 shows their balance sheet holds $192.2 million in cash and equivalents. They also have $81.6 million in financial liabilities, which I'll consider as debt for simplicity. That way, I estimate CLLS's enterprise value at around $251.8 million. According to Seeking Alpha's dashboard on CLLS, they're projected to generate $73.8 million by 2026. And this alone implies a cheap forward EV/S of just 3.4. For comparison, its sector's median forward EV/S is closer to 4.0.

Similarly, I calculate CLLS' latest quarterly cash burn at $4.0 million during Q3 2025. Note that I got this figure by simply adding its cash flows from operations and CAPEX, but I also included purchases of intangible assets since it's a biotech. This would imply a manageable annual cash burn run rate of roughly $16.0 million. It also suggests a comfortable cash runway of 12.0 years, though management guides for a more conservative target of 2H2027. This is likely because they anticipate their cash burn will increase as their clinical trials scale.

Moreover, you can think of CLLS as having two broad value verticals. Personally, I think the main bet with CLLS is for its in-house assets, particularly UCART22. However, this is clearly still a few years away from reaching any FDA approvals. After all, its most advanced trials are still Phase 1. However, UCART22 (or lasme-cel) alone could reach peak sales of $700 million by 2035 (contingent on regulatory approvals, of course). And given CLLS's runway and promising trial data, I feel this angle is sufficient to justify a long-term bullish view on the stock.

But additionally, CLLS also has interesting partnerships with Allogene, Servier, Iovance (IOVA), and AstraZeneca (AZN). Combined, these collaborations/partnerships provide CLLS with a compelling stream of potential milestone payments and licensing fees over time. After all, their balance sheet shows around $94.0 million in deferred revenues/income, which CLLS should realize over the next few years as their partnership R&D progresses. And if these collaborations lead to a drug approval, CLLS will also be eligible for royalty payments.

In other words, even if their in-house assets fail, the partnerships can still potentially lead to recurrent royalties. Naturally, this is also the main risk with CLLS. If their R&D ultimately fails and none of its assets (in-house or collaborations) succeed, then CLLS could lead to substantial shareholder losses. Yet, this is an inherent risk in biotechs, and I don't think this is an above-average concern for CLLS stock based on its promising trial data so far. And obviously, CLLS is a microcap, which makes it much more volatile than larger caps in this sector. In fact, it has only 171.6 thousand shares in volume, which at the current $5.30 PPS, implies a very shallow trading liquidity of just $0.9 million. Hence, I would imagine even small buy/sell orders can push the price significantly.

Finally, CLLS appears to be involved in a patent lawsuit related to its TALEN-based approach to gene-editing. However, as far as I can tell, this is still a very early-stage litigation. So, I would imagine that this overhang could only play out on a multi-year timeframe. I'm also not a lawyer, so I don't have more insight on this, but remember that patent litigations are common in biotech. Thus, for now, I consider this recent lawsuit as "part of doing business" for CLLS, rather than a major risk. Still, if an update shows this lawsuit has real teeth, then CLLS could also trade much lower, so keep that tail risk in mind.

Conclusion: Reiterated "Buy"

Overall, I'm still very bullish on CLLS over the long-term, mostly based on their prospects with lasma-cel. I also think they have compelling optionality with their partnerships, and their cash runway gives them ample room for maneuvering. Just remember this remains a microcap biotech that ultimately depends on regulatory approvals, which is inherently highly speculative.
